sections.header: Verses 1-16 — The Exile Baggage and the Blinded Prince

heb.paragraph: Ezekiel packs a deportation bag in daylight — a knapsack with only the barest essentials — then digs through the mud-brick wall of his house at evening and carries his bag out with his face covered. The sign-act prophesies Zedekiah's attempted escape from Jerusalem in 586 BC, when the king fled through a breach in the wall and was captured.

panels.ctx: This sign-act specifically targets King Zedekiah, 'the prince in Jerusalem' (v.10). Zedekiah attempted to flee Jerusalem at night through a gap in the wall but was captured near Jericho, blinded, and taken to Babylon in chains (2 Kgs 25:4-7). Ezekiel's prophecy is remarkably precise: the covered face (v.6) corresponds to Zedekiah's blinding, and the failed escape (v.13) matches the historical outcome exactly.

cross.note: Zedekiah fled through the wall at night, was captured, blinded, and taken to Babylon — the exact fulfillment of Ezekiel's sign-act.
Jeremiah's parallel account of Zedekiah's capture, blinding, and deportation.
The detailed account of Zedekiah's escape attempt and punishment.

notes.note: Rebellious house with eyes that do not see and ears that do not hear — spiritual blindness that will become physical blindness for Zedekiah.
Face covered so he cannot see the ground — a prophetic detail fulfilled when Nebuchadnezzar had Zedekiah's eyes put out before deporting him.
The prince will carry his belongings on his shoulder and his face will be covered. God's net will catch him — he will be brought to Babylon but will not see it.
A few will be spared to confess their abominations among the nations. Even in scattering, God preserves witnesses.

Calvin highlights the public nature of the sign-act: done 'in their sight' because words alone have failed. God accommodates his message to the stubbornness of the audience — if they will not hear, perhaps they will see.
The 'prince' (nasi) in Jerusalem is Zedekiah — Ezekiel avoids the title 'king' (melek), possibly because Jehoiachin is still considered the legitimate king in exile.
The net and snare imagery is hunting language — God is the hunter and Zedekiah is the prey. The captured king will be taken to Babylon but will not 'see' it — a prediction of his blinding.

Block emphasises the theatrical quality: Ezekiel is a one-man drama company, performing the exile of Zedekiah before it happens. The exiles watch their prophet dig through a wall with his face covered and recognize it as street theater with deadly serious content. Prophecy becomes performance art.

Zimmerli argues the interpretation (vv.10-16) was added after the events of 586 BC to demonstrate fulfillment. The original sign-act (vv.1-7) was ambiguous; the commentary retrojects the specificity of Zedekiah's actual fate onto the prophetic action.

heb.paragraph: The people quote a proverb: 'The days go by and every vision comes to nothing.' This cynical mashal dismisses prophetic warnings as empty threats. God responds by abolishing the proverb: the word will be fulfilled without delay. No more postponement, no more patience.

panels.ctx: Two related oracles address prophetic cynicism. The first (vv.17-20) is a sign-act: eating and drinking with trembling to depict the terror of siege conditions. The second (vv.21-28) confronts the proverb 'the days drag on and every vision fails.' The people had grown numb to prophetic warnings — decades of threatened judgment without fulfillment bred contempt. God's answer: the delay is over.

cross.note: Scoffers will say: 'Where is the promise of his coming? Everything goes on as it has since the beginning.' Peter addresses the same cynicism Ezekiel confronts.
The vision awaits its appointed time; it will not prove false. Though it linger, wait for it — it will certainly come.

notes.note: The proverb 'the days go by and every vision comes to nothing' reveals deep cynicism. The people interpreted the delay of judgment as cancellation of judgment.
God's answer is absolute: 'I the LORD will speak, and whatever word I speak will be performed. It will no longer be delayed.' Divine patience has a limit.
The people reinterpret prophecy as applying to the distant future — 'he prophesies about times far away.' God refuses the deferral: 'None of my words will be delayed any longer.'

Calvin applies this to every generation that mocks prophetic warnings: delay is not cancellation. God's patience in withholding judgment is mistaken for weakness, but when the appointed time comes, every word will be fulfilled.
The proverb format (mashal) indicates this was a popular saying circulating among the exiles and perhaps in Jerusalem — a folk theology of divine inaction.
God will 'put an end to' (shavat) the proverb — the same verb used for Sabbath rest, here meaning 'to cease.' The false proverb will be silenced by fulfillment.

Block identifies the two proverbs (v.22 and v.27) as representing two forms of resistance to prophecy: outright denial ('every vision fails') and temporal deferral ('it applies to the distant future'). Both strategies neutralize the prophetic word. God rejects both.

Zimmerli sees this oracle as addressing the fundamental crisis of prophecy: if a prophetic word is not fulfilled promptly, does it lose authority? Ezekiel insists that divine timing does not undermine divine truth. The delay was mercy; its end is justice.

rows.label: vv.1-7
rows.text: Sign-Act: Exile Baggage Through the Wall

lit.note: Two sign-acts and two oracles: the exile of Zedekiah and the end of prophetic delay. Performance and proclamation together.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the chapter is about the authority and fulfillment of the prophetic word against cynical dismissal. Judgment at 9: the prophecies concern imminent destruction.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Sign-Acts of Exile

translations.text: “Therefore, son of man, pack your belongings for exile and in the daytime, as they watch, set out and go from where you are to another place. Perhaps they will understand, though they are a rebellious people.

As for you, son of man, prepare for yourself an exile’s baggage, and go into exile by day in their sight. You shall go like an exile from your place to another place in their sight. Perhaps they will understand, though they are a rebellious house.
Put them on your shoulder as they are watching and carry them out at dusk. Cover your face so that you cannot see the land, for I have made you a sign to the Israelites.”
In their sight you shall lift the baggage upon your shoulder and carry it out at dusk. You shall cover your face that you may not see the land, for I have made you a sign for the house of Israel.”
